{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"# Overview of the Data Quality Assessment Methodology 
Identifies key data quality issues by assessing indicator completeness, detecting extreme outliers, and evaluating consistency between related indicators — using monthly, facility-level HMIS data.

Applies targeted adjustments to flagged data points by replacing outliers and imputing missing values using a centered 12-month rolling average; fallback facility-level averages are used when insufficient history exists.

Enables sensitivity analysis by producing results under four scenarios (no adjustment, outlier-only, completeness-only, and combined), with certain indicators that don't meet inclusion criteria excluded from adjustment. For this analysis, adjustment is for both outliers and completeness.

# Overview of the Service Utilization Assessment Methodology 
Service utilization trend analysis, which identifies the percentage change in service use for each quarter of data compared to the previous quarter.

Disruptions and surplus in service delivery utilization analysis, which helps to identify meaningful positive or negative changes in service use outside of what we would expect given seasonal trends and historical trends.

# Overview of the Service Coverage Estimation Methodology
Coverage estimation analysis uses routine data to estimate trends in service coverage at national and sub-national levels. This occurs by integrating adjusted health service volume data, population projections, and survey data (MICS/DHS). Coverage estimates are calculated for key health indicators using multiple denominator sources, and the optimal denominator is selected by minimizing the error relative to the most recent survey data."}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"More details on the methodology and data quality adjustment approaches are found in the Annex. All R code and source documentation is also publicly available on GitHub (https://github.com/FASTR-Analytics)."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Methodology"}}

Service Volume Trends
- Consistent growth trajectory: All vaccines show an upward trend from Q1 2024  to Q1 2025 , with Q1 2025 representing peak performance across all indicators
- Seasonal pattern: Notable dip in Q2 2024 across all vaccines, suggesting potential seasonal factors or temporary disruptions
- Recovery and stabilization: Post-Q1 recovery followed by stabilization in Q2-Q3 2025

- Penta Vaccine 1: Significant underperformance in Q2 2024 (-4.7%), but perfect alignment thereafter
- Penta Vaccine 3: Excellent performance with actual matching expected values throughout

Both antenatal care services show stable utilization (60K-82K for ANC1; 36K-45K for ANC4) with slight upward trajectory in 2025. ANC1 exhibits higher volatility than ANC4.

Significant drop-off from first visit (ANC1) to fourth visit (ANC4), averaging 40% attrition, indicating incomplete care continuum.

Recommendations

1. Strengthen retention programs** between ANC1-ANC4 to reduce 40% attrition rate

2. Investigate volatility causes in ANC1 monthly fluctuations for resource planning

3. {"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","markdown":"# Methodology: Service Utilization Assessment","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"# Purpose: 
Track changes in health service use over time, identifying where services fall below or rise above expected patterns.

# How it works:
- Uses routine HMIS data, cleaned for outliers and missing values.
- Builds an “expected” trend line for each service, adjusting for seasonality and historical trends in service utilization.
- Compares actual service volumes to expected levels.


# Measuring impact:
- Flagged disruption periods are analyzed to estimate how much service volumes changed compared to what was expected.
- Results are shown at nationaland sub-national levels highlighting both system-wide and localized effects.


# How to interpret figures
- Red shaded areas = potential disruptions (service volumes lower than expected).
- Green shaded areas = potential surpluses (service volumes higher than expected).
- These are signals, not conclusions — they highlight when and where volumes deviate, but require further investigation into the underlying reasons (“why”)."}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"More details on the methodology and data quality adjustment approaches are found alongside the source code on GitHub (https://github.com/FASTR-Analytics)."}]],"useHeader":false,"headerText":"Methodology"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","span":4,"markdown":"# Summary of Completeness Trends (July 2023 – December 2025)

Overall completeness: Describe trends in overall completeness

Areas of weaker completeness: Highlight indicators with completeness below 90%

Improvement in 2025: Describe indicator completeness over time

# Why Completeness Matters for the Disruptions Analysis

Observed values: These are adjusted for outliers only, so they reflect the actual raw service volumes after removing implausible spikes.

Expected values: These are adjusted for both completeness and outliers. This means the model “fills in” where reporting gaps exist, building an expected trend line as if all facilities had reported consistently.

When completeness is high, observed and expected volumes are more comparable, and disruptions are more likely to reflect true service changes.

When completeness is low, expected values may be artificially higher than observed, creating apparent “disruptions” that actually reflect missing reports rather than real declines in service delivery.","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.64,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false},{"type":"figure","presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"4bd741c0-8bf3-4e4c-a0a4-5528426d29c3","moduleId":"m001","isDefault":false,"selectedReplicantValue":""},"stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":true,"figureAdditionalScale":0.7,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}]],"useHeader":false,"headerText":"Completeness is >95% for most indicators in 2025, strengthening confidence in disruption findings"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"# What is data quality assessment?
The Data Quality Assessment (DQA) methodology applies three core assessments: detection of extreme outliers in reported service volumes, analysis of indicator-specific completeness, and consistency checks between related indicators. For each facility and month, the data are processed to identify anomalies, assess reporting continuity, and validate internal coherence across indicators. Outliers are flagged using Median Absolute Deviation (MAD) methods and proportional thresholds. Completeness is evaluated based on whether facilities report non-zero values across months for core indicators. Consistency is checked across linked indicators (e.g., ANC1 vs ANC4, Penta1 vs Penta3), using predefined ratio thresholds. The results are integrated into a composite DQA score that summarizes whether a facility's data meet all quality benchmarks.

# What is data quality adjustment? 
Data quality adjustment is the process of correcting known issues in routine health data to improve the reliability of analyses. In the FASTR approach, adjustments are applied to HMIS data using automated methods that identify and correct outlier values and impute missing data based on patterns in historical reporting. This allows countries to make more robust use of the data they already have, even when reporting is imperfect.

# Why adjust for data quality?
Routine health data, such as those captured in DHIS2, are an essential resource for monitoring and improving primary health care systems. However, data quality issues — like outliers, incomplete reporting, and inconsistencies between related indicators — can distort analyses and misinform decision-making. 
The FASTR approach includes built-in statistical methods to identify and adjust for these issues, producing more robust and actionable results. While not all data quality challenges can be fully resolved, targeted adjustments improve the reliability of the data and help ensure it can still inform decisions. Rather than treating poor data quality as a barrier, FASTR transforms it into an opportunity for feedback and improvement, enabling countries to use the data they have to make timely, evidence-based decisions.

# How was adjustment done?
The Data Quality Adjustment module uses HMIS data together with the outlier and completeness flags generated by the Data Quality Assessment module, which identify where service volumes are unusually high or missing. Outlier values are replaced with a centered 12-month facility-level rolling average based on valid (non-flagged) data. Missing values due to incomplete reporting are imputed using the same method. If there is not enough valid historical data, a fallback average at the facility level is used instead. Adjustments are applied under four scenarios - no adjustment, outlier-only, completeness-only, and both combined - to enable sensitivity analysis. Indicators which don't meet minimum volume thresholds are excluded from adjustment.
For this analysis, adjustment is for outliers and completeness."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Methodology: Data Quality Adjustment Approach"}}

### 1. **Family Planning Services Trend**

**Overall Pattern:**
- Service volume shows steady growth from ~45,000-55,000 (2020) to 55,000-60,000 acceptors (2024-2025)
- Represents approximately **20% increase** over the period
- Relatively stable month-to-month variation (±5,000-10,000)

**Notable Disruptions:**
- **May 2020 (periodId 1444)**: Significant deficit of ~6,000 acceptors (44,107 actual vs. 50,265 expected) - likely COVID-19 impact
- **October 2021 (periodId 1458)**: Major shortfall of ~10,000 acceptors (43,910 vs. 54,201 expected)
- **November 2021 (periodId 1459)**: Continued deficit of ~9,000 acceptors
- **October 2022 (periodId 1481)**: Deficit of ~6,000 acceptors (51,927 vs. 57,991 expected)

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"Analysis of routine health management information systems data (HMIS) and the methodological choices contained in this report provide strengths and limitations which are important to consider in the interpretation of the results."}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"# Strengths 
- Leverages routinely collected HMIS data, enabling timely and low-cost monitoring.
- Includes rigorous data quality assessment and adjustment to improve reliability of estimates.
- Enables subnational analysis for granular insights.
- Supports policy-relevant questions through tailored indicator selection and flexible stratification.
- Accounts for seasonality and historical trends using statistical methods not available in DHIS2.
- Complements existing DHIS2 tools and strengthens data use through feedback loops."},{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"# Limitations
- Cannot correct for all types of data quality issues (e.g., systematic underreporting, denominator inaccuracies).
- Does not account for facilities that never report, which may bias results in areas with large reporting gaps.
- Low-volume indicators are more sensitive to anomalies and may not be adjusted.
- Requires consistent indicator definitions and stable reporting systems over time for trend analysis."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Strengths and limitations of this analysis"}}

 Key Findings
**Overall Trend: Declining Data Quality**
All four provinces show concerning patterns, with three experiencing significant deterioration in data quality scores by 2025.

Province-Specific Performance
**1. Lusaka (Best Performer)**
- Maintains the highest scores across all years (80-83%)
- Shows resilience: improved in 2024 (82.8%) before slight decline in 2025 (80.1%)
- Only province sustaining adequate quality above 80% threshold

**2. Eastern Province**
- Started strong (77.6% in 2023)
- **Critical decline in 2025**: dropped to 65.2% (-12.4 percentage points from 2023)
- Most dramatic deterioration among all provinces

**3. Southern Province**
- Consistent downward trajectory
- Declined from 78.0% (2023) to 66.0% (2025)
- Lost 12 percentage points over two years

**4. Western Province (Highest Risk)**
- Consistently lowest performer (46-61% range)
- **Severe quality issues**: Only 46.4% in 2024, slight recovery to 49.5% in 2025
- More than half of facility-months have inadequate data quality

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","markdown":"# Health Facility Service Volume Interpretation

### 1. **Sustained Growth in Antenatal Care 4 (ANC4) Coverage**
ANC4 services show a strong upward trend, increasing from ~114,700 in Q4 2023 to ~131,000 in Q3 2025 (+14% growth). This represents improved quality of maternal care, as more women are completing the recommended four antenatal visits. The consistent quarter-over-quarter growth, particularly from Q0 2025 onwards, suggests successful program implementation and improved patient retention.

### 2. **Stable but Fluctuating First Antenatal Visit (ANC1) Volumes**
ANC1 services remain relatively stable around 200,000-220,000 visits throughout the period, with moderate fluctuations. The slight upward trend from Q9 2023 (~207,000) to Q2 2025 (~223,600) indicates steady demand for initial maternal health services, though growth is less pronounced than ANC4.

### 3. **Improving ANC Completion Rate Signals Program Effectiveness**
The gap between ANC1 and ANC4 is narrowing over time (from ~85,600 difference in Q4 2023 to ~83,300 in Q3 2025), with ANC4 growing faster than ANC1. This suggests improved continuity of care and better patient follow-through, indicating strengthened health system capacity to retain pregnant women through their full antenatal care journey—a critical health system management success metric.","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false},{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"834eed88-4b67-475e-a907-fe255d2515a8","moduleId":"m003","isDefault":false,"selectedReplicantValue":""}}]]}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"647c671f-d005-4497-84c6-06290ee9f559","moduleId":"m003","isDefault":false,"selectedReplicantValue":""}},{"type":"text","markdown":"**Overall Pattern**: Service utilization demonstrates moderate fluctuation throughout the year, ranging from approximately 45,700 to 61,100 services, with an average around 56,500 services per month.

### Notable Findings

1. **Peak Performance Period (Aug-Sep 2025)**
   - Highest utilization recorded in September (61,118 services)
   - August also shows strong performance (60,461 services)
   - This represents a ~14% increase above the yearly average

2. **Concerning Decline (Oct-Nov 2025)**
   - Sharp drop beginning in October (51,210 services, -16% from September)
   - Further deterioration in November (45,703 services, -25% from peak)
   - This represents the lowest utilization in the tracked period","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}]],"useFooter":true}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"1bdeb30f-fdfb-4083-af3e-e989f38e4cbc","moduleId":"m003","isDefault":false,"selectedReplicantValue":""}},{"type":"text","markdown":"BCG and Penta Vaccine 1 services show broadly stable utilization with strong seasonal alignment, indicating synchronized service delivery influenced by system-wide factors rather than antigen-specific issues, while recurring declines of approximately 5–10 percent are consistently observed in June and November/December, suggesting potential mid-year and end-year service disruptions. Penta 1 volumes are slightly lower than BCG, as expected, and demonstrate modest improvement in 2025, including a notable peak in November 2024. In contrast, Penta Vaccine 3 exhibits lower and more volatile performance, remaining consistently 2,000–5,000 doses below Penta 1 and indicating an estimated 3–8 percent dropout between first and third doses, with gaps widening during low-performance months. Health Facility Services Analysis (2024-2025)

Service delivery shows moderate volatility with volumes ranging from 59,500 to 81,600 across all adjustment methods. A notable dip occurred in June 2024 (period 1493: ~59,500 services), representing the lowest point—potentially indicating reporting gaps or service disruptions.

August 2025 demonstrates peak performance (~81,600 services), suggesting improved capacity or seasonal demand. The data reveals minimal differences between adjustment methods (typically <1-2%), indicating good baseline data quality with few outliers.
